quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cell cycle checkpoints"?

options.text: S = cAᶻ; the number of species increases with habitat area, where z ≈ 0.3 for islands and c is a constant
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis; increases genetic variation
Control points at G1, G2, and M where the cell evaluates conditions before proceeding; can halt the cycle if conditions are unfavorable
The main replication enzyme in E. coli; synthesizes DNA 5'→3' by adding nucleotides complementary to the template strand

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cell cycle checkpoints" is defined as: Control points at G1, G2, and M where the cell evaluates conditions before proceeding; can halt the cycle if conditions are unfavorable. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cyclin-CDK complexes"?

options.text: Small intracellular molecules (cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP₃) that relay and amplify signals from the receptor to target proteins
Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap
A series of protein kinases that sequentially phosphorylate and activate each other, amplifying the signal at each step
Cyclins accumulate and activate CDKs at specific cell cycle stages, driving progression through phosphorylation of target proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cyclin-CDK complexes" is defined as: Cyclins accumulate and activate CDKs at specific cell cycle stages, driving progression through phosphorylation of target proteins. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Tumor suppressor gene"?

options.text: The monomer of proteins: a central carbon bonded to an amino group (-NH₂), carboxyl group (-COOH), hydrogen, and variable R-group
The totality of an organism's chemical reactions; catabolism (breakdown, releases energy) and anabolism (synthesis, requires energy)
A structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls made of β-glucose linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds; forms rigid, indigestible fibers
A gene encoding a protein that inhibits cell division (e.g., p53 triggers apoptosis in damaged cells, Rb blocks G1/S transition)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Tumor suppressor gene" is defined as: A gene encoding a protein that inhibits cell division (e.g., p53 triggers apoptosis in damaged cells, Rb blocks G1/S transition). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Proto-oncogene"?

options.text: A normal gene that promotes cell growth; mutations can convert it to an oncogene, causing uncontrolled cell division
A mathematical model estimating a population's probability of persistence over a specified time period, incorporating demographic and environmental variability
A series of molecular events by which a signal on a cell's surface is converted to a specific cellular response
The monomer of proteins: a central carbon bonded to an amino group (-NH₂), carboxyl group (-COOH), hydrogen, and variable R-group

quiz_questions.explanation: "Proto-oncogene" is defined as: A normal gene that promotes cell growth; mutations can convert it to an oncogene, causing uncontrolled cell division. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Density-dependent inhibition"?

options.text: Normal cells stop dividing when they contact neighboring cells; cancer cells lose this control (contact inhibition)
A series of molecular events by which a signal on a cell's surface is converted to a specific cellular response
A measure of disorder or randomness; the 2nd law of thermodynamics states that entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes
The percentage of offspring showing recombinant phenotypes; used to calculate the distance between linked genes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Density-dependent inhibition" is defined as: Normal cells stop dividing when they contact neighboring cells; cancer cells lose this control (contact inhibition). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Control points at G1, G2, and M where the cell evaluates conditions before proceeding; can halt the cycle if conditions are unfavorable"?

options.text: Allopatric speciation
Keystone species
Cell cycle checkpoints
FADH₂

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cell cycle checkpoints".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Cyclins accumulate and activate CDKs at specific cell cycle stages, driving progression through phosphorylation of target proteins"?

options.text: Codominance
Cyclin-CDK complexes
Vestigial structures
Chitin

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cyclin-CDK complexes".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A gene encoding a protein that inhibits cell division (e.g., p53 triggers apoptosis in damaged cells, Rb blocks G1/S transition)"?

options.text: r-selected species
Tumor suppressor gene
Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)
Phospholipid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Tumor suppressor gene".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A normal gene that promotes cell growth; mutations can convert it to an oncogene, causing uncontrolled cell division"?

options.text: Proto-oncogene
Pre-mRNA processing
Allele frequency
Energy coupling

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Proto-oncogene".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Normal cells stop dividing when they contact neighboring cells; cancer cells lose this control (contact inhibition)"?

options.text: Net primary productivity (NPP)
Biodiversity hotspot
Density-dependent inhibition
Recombination frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Density-dependent inhibition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Regulation of Cell Cycle is accurate?

options.text: The central dogma of molecular biology—DNA → RNA → Protein—describes the flow of genetic information in cells.
DNA Replication and Transcription describes how genetic information stored in DNA is copied (replication) and read into mRNA (transcription).
Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid (2n) daughter cells for growth and repair, while meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid (n) gametes for sexual reproduction.
Regulation of Cell Cycle ensures orderly progression through G1, S, G2, and M phases via internal checkpoints and external signals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Cell Cycle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: Population Ecology studies how populations grow, interact with their environment, and are regulated.

Eukaryotic cells compartmentalize functions in membrane-bound organelles: the nucleus (DNA storage), endoplasmic reticulum (protein/lipid synthesis), Golgi apparatus (modification and sorting), mitochondria (ATP production), and chloroplasts...
Cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) activated by cyclins drive progression past checkpoints.
Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid (2n) daughter cells for growth and repair, while meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid (n) gametes for sexual reproduction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Cell Cycle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: Enzyme specificity arises from the precise 3D shape of the active site, which is determined by the amino acid sequence.

In eukaryotes, regulation occurs at multiple levels: chromatin remodeling (acetylation, methylation), transcription factors, mRNA processing, mRNA stability, translation regulation, and post-translational modification.

The G1 checkpoint (restriction point) commits the cell to division, the G2 checkpoint verifies complete DNA replication, and the M checkpoint (spindle assembly checkpoint) ensures proper chromosome attachment.
Energy and Metabolism describes how cells manage energy through coupled reactions and metabolic pathways.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Cell Cycle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: Exponential growth (dN/dt = rN) occurs when resources are unlimited, producing J-shaped curves.
Tumor suppressors (p53, Rb) and proto-oncogenes regulate these checkpoints.
While most mutations are neutral or harmful, some confer advantages (e.g., sickle cell heterozygote advantage against malaria).
Triglycerides (fats and oils) store energy (9 kcal/g) through ester bonds between glycerol and three fatty acids.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Cell Cycle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: The fossil record shows transitional forms (Tiktaalik between fish and tetrapods), comparative anatomy reveals homologous structures (bat wing, whale flipper, human arm sharing the same bone pattern), molecular biology shows DNA/protein sequence...
Monohybrid crosses (Aa × Aa → 3:1 phenotypic ratio) and dihybrid crosses (AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 ratio) predict offspring ratios, testable with chi-square analysis.
Logistic growth (dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K) incorporates carrying capacity K and produces S-shaped curves.
Regulation of Cell Cycle ensures orderly progression through G1, S, G2, and M phases via internal checkpoints and external signals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Cell Cycle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: p53 ('guardian of the genome') mutations found in over 50% of human cancers, allowing damaged cells to continue dividing rather than undergoing apoptosis
Metabolic interconnections: Krebs cycle intermediates serve as precursors for amino acid synthesis, fatty acid synthesis, and gluconeogenesis
Genetic counseling using Punnett squares and pedigree analysis to calculate the probability of offspring inheriting genetic diseases like cystic fibrosis
Colon cancer progression following the multistep model: APC loss → K-ras activation → DCC loss → p53 loss → metastatic cancer over 10-15 years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Cell Cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: Ecosystem services valuation estimating that pollination by insects is worth $235-577 billion annually globally, providing economic justification for pollinator conservation
HPV (human papillomavirus) causing cervical cancer by producing proteins that inactivate p53 and Rb tumor suppressors, disabling cell cycle checkpoints
Genetic counseling using Punnett squares and pedigree analysis to calculate the probability of offspring inheriting genetic diseases like cystic fibrosis
α-amanitin (death cap mushroom toxin) inhibiting RNA polymerase II, blocking transcription and causing fatal liver failure within days

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Cell Cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Regulation of Cell Cycle applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Cancer immunotherapy using checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4) to re-enable the immune system's ability to attack cancer cells
Down syndrome (trisomy 21) caused by nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in an extra copy of chromosome 21 in all cells
Virus tracking during COVID-19 pandemic using SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing to build phylogenetic trees tracing transmission chains and identifying new variants
Genetic counseling using Punnett squares and pedigree analysis to calculate the probability of offspring inheriting genetic diseases like cystic fibrosis

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Cell Cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Regulation of Cell Cycle?

options.text: ABO blood type compatibility in transfusions determined by codominant Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles and recessive i allele (3 alleles, 6 genotypes, 4 phenotypes)
Feedback inhibition in the cholesterol synthesis pathway: statins mimic the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase, acting as competitive inhibitors to lower cholesterol levels
p53 ('guardian of the genome') mutations found in over 50% of human cancers, allowing damaged cells to continue dividing rather than undergoing apoptosis
Amazon deforestation averaging ~10,000 km² per year, threatening the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystem with an estimated 10% of all species on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Cell Cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Regulation of Cell Cycle in practice?

options.text: Darwin's finch beak evolution on Daphne Major Island: drought years selected for larger beaks (to crack harder seeds), directly observed by Peter and Rosemary Grant over 40 years
HPV vaccines (Gardasil) preventing cervical cancer by protecting against viral proteins (E6, E7) that inactivate tumor suppressors p53 and Rb
HPV (human papillomavirus) causing cervical cancer by producing proteins that inactivate p53 and Rb tumor suppressors, disabling cell cycle checkpoints
Antibiotic targeting of bacterial ribosomes: tetracycline blocks the A-site, chloramphenicol inhibits peptidyl transferase—both exploit differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Cell Cycle.
